Question: For calculating magnetic fields, what are the advantages and disadvantages of the Biot-Savart law?
Answer: Biot-Savart law’s advantage is that it works with any magnetic field produced by a current loop. The disadvantage is that it can take a long time.

Question: Describe the magnetic field due to the current in two wires connected to the two terminals of a source of emf and twisted tightly around each other.
How can you decide if a wire is infinite?
Answer: If you were to go to the start of a line segment and calculate the angle $$\theta$$ to be approximately $$0 \text{°}$$ , the wire can be considered infinite. This judgment is based also on the precision you need in the result.

Question: Identical currents are carried in two circular loops; however, one loop has twice the diameter as the other loop. Compare the magnetic fields created by the loops at the center of each loop.

Topic: 12.2 Magnetic Field Due to a Thin Straight Wire
Question: How would you orient two long, straight, current-carrying wires so that there is no net magnetic force between them? ( Hint : What orientation would lead to one wire not experiencing a magnetic field from the other?)
Answer: You would make sure the currents flow perpendicular to one another.

Topic: 12.3 Magnetic Force between Two Parallel Currents
Question: Compare and contrast the electric field of an infinite line of charge and the magnetic field of an infinite line of current.

Topic: 12.3 Magnetic Force between Two Parallel Currents
Question: Is $$\mathbf{\vec{B}}$$ constant in magnitude for points that lie on a magnetic field line?
Answer: A magnetic field line gives the direction of the magnetic field at any point in space. The density of magnetic field lines indicates the strength of the magnetic field.

Topic: 12.4 Magnetic Field of a Current Loop
Question: Is the magnetic field of a current loop uniform?

Topic: 12.4 Magnetic Field of a Current Loop
Question: What happens to the length of a suspended spring when a current passes through it?
Answer: The spring reduces in length since each coil will have a north pole-produced magnetic field next to a south pole of the next coil.

Topic: 12.4 Magnetic Field of a Current Loop
Question: Two concentric circular wires with different diameters carry currents in the same direction. Describe the force on the inner wire.
Is Ampère’s law valid for all closed paths? Why isn’t it normally useful for calculating a magnetic field?
Answer: Ampère’s law is valid for all closed paths, but it is not useful for calculating fields when the magnetic field produced lacks symmetry that can be exploited by a suitable choice of path.

Question: Is the magnetic field inside a toroid completely uniform? Almost uniform?
Explain why $$\mathbf{\vec{B}} = 0$$ inside a long, hollow copper pipe that is carrying an electric current parallel to the axis. Is $$\mathbf{\vec{B}} = 0$$ outside the pipe?
Answer: If there is no current inside the loop, there is no magnetic field (see Ampère’s law). Outside the pipe, there may be an enclosed current through the copper pipe, so the magnetic field may not be zero outside the pipe.

Question: A diamagnetic material is brought close to a permanent magnet. What happens to the material?
If you cut a bar magnet into two pieces, will you end up with one magnet with an isolated north pole and another magnet with an isolated south pole? Explain your answer.
Answer: The bar magnet will then become two magnets, each with their own north and south poles. There are no magnetic monopoles or single pole magnets.
